title: Cat Falling to the Side While Walking: Causes and Emergency Steps
meta_desc: If your cat is falling to the side, it may be a serious neurological or vestibular issue. Learn why this requires immediate veterinary attention.
content_html:
What is happening to my cat?

When a cat falls to the side while walking, it is often a sign of ataxia or vestibular syndrome. This occurs when there is a disruption in the body's system for maintaining balance. It can be caused by issues within the inner ear, such as an infection, or problems within the central nervous system, such as a stroke, tumor, or inflammatory disease.

Is this an emergency?

Yes, the urgency level is High. A sudden loss of balance or coordination is a significant clinical sign that should never be ignored. Your cat may also experience nausea, rapid eye movements (nystagmus), or a head tilt. Because these symptoms can mimic life-threatening conditions like poisoning or cardiovascular events, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How a photo or video helps triage

Providing your veterinarian with a short video of your cat attempting to walk is incredibly helpful for a rapid diagnosis. While a photo can show a head tilt or abnormal posture, a video allows the vet to see the specific 'drunken' gait and any involuntary eye movements. This visual evidence helps the triage team determine if the issue is likely localized to the ear or if it is a more complex neurological emergency.

faqs.question: What is vestibular disease in cats?
faqs.answer: Vestibular disease is a sudden, non-progressive disturbance of balance. It can be 'idiopathic,' meaning the cause is unknown, but it must be distinguished from more serious infections or tumors.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause my cat to fall over?
faqs.answer: Yes, a deep infection in the middle or inner ear can disrupt the vestibular system, leading to a loss of balance and falling to one side.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human medicine for dizziness?
faqs.answer: No, never give your cat human medications. Many are toxic to pets. Your veterinarian will prescribe safe, feline-specific medications for nausea and inflammation.
